That makes sense because if there's abnormal biting force, especially on the margins of the gums where teeth meet the soft tissues, that area might get traumatized. Like maybe from sharp edges or uneven surfaces. So A seems likely to be true.

Option B states "Reversible." If the trauma is due to things like plaque buildup or minor irregularities that can be addressed by professional cleaning or adjusting the dentition, then yes, reversing those factors might make the issue resolve itself. So B sounds plausible too.

Option C mentions "Reduction in height of alveolar crest." Alveolar crests are parts of the jawbone that support the teeth. Prolonged stress from occlusion could potentially resorb some of this bone over time, leading to decreased height. Therefore, C also seems accurate.

Periodontal pockets form mainly due to infection and inflammation, not directly from occlusal trauma alone. However, if there's persistent occlusal trauma combined with bacterial accumulation, could it lead to deeper structures developing into pockets? But perhaps in pure occlusal trauma scenarios without infection, you wouldn't see actual pockets formed yet. So stating "No pocket" as a characteristic might mean that this specific kind of trauma doesn't inherently cause pockets themselves—it requires additional pathologic changes.
